preFix.title: Fitting a dust cover to the underside of a chair
preFix.subtitle: Staple a black cambric dust cover across the underside of an upholstered chair frame
preFix.excerpt: How to fit a black cambric or non-woven dust cover across the underside of an upholstered chair, covering cutting to size, folding the edges, and stapling neatly around the perimeter of the frame to give a clean finished underside.
preFix.sourceNotes: The Practical Upholsterer (1917). Dix and Miller Modern Upholstery (1948). Cassell's Household Guide (upholstery).

content.text: is always the last piece fitted on an upholstered chair, after all the covering work is complete. It is a simple finish: a rectangle of black cambric or non-woven fabric, folded at the edges and stapled around the bottom of the frame rails. The fold at the edge prevents fraying and gives a neat line where the fabric meets the wood.

Black cambric (a woven cotton) or a black non-woven dust cover fabric, available from upholstery suppliers by the metre. Measure the underside of the chair from outside rail to outside rail in both directions and add 50 mm to each measurement for the fold-under. T50 staples in 6 mm length for the dust cover layer.
Step 1, remove the old dust cover

Use the staple remover to lift each staple from the underside of the frame. The old dust cover may be tacked rather than stapled on older chairs: lift the tacks with the staple-remover tip. Once the cover is off, check the webbing and any springs for damage. The dust cover is the last job, so any webbing or spring repairs must be done before fitting the new cover.
Step 2, cut and fold the fabric

Cut the cambric to the measured size plus the 50 mm fold allowance on each side. Fold the edges under by 25 mm all around the perimeter and press the folds flat with the fingers. The folded edge is what gets stapled to the frame: the fold prevents any frayed edge showing on the outside of the chair.
Step 3, staple the dust cover to the frame

Flip the chair upside down on the bench. Hold the cambric in position with the folded edges against the inside face of the frame rails. Start at the centre of the back rail and drive one staple through the folded edge into the rail. Pull the fabric taut to the front rail and staple at its centre. Repeat for both side rails. Work outward from the centre staples on each rail, pulling the fabric smooth and driving staples at 50 mm centres. Staple around the leg positions: cut a small V-notch into the fold at each corner to allow the fabric to sit flat against the rail.
Step 4, trim and check the corners

At each corner, fold the cambric neatly and trim away any excess so the fold lies flat against the corner of the frame. A single diagonal cut from the corner of the fold to the corner of the leg notch removes the excess. Drive a staple through the folded corner to hold it. Check the underside of the chair: the cambric should be taut and even with no visible sags between staples.

On a chair with exposed wooden legs and no rails (a stool or occasional chair), the dust cover is fixed to the underside of the seat pad rather than to the rails. Fold the fabric under and staple across the pad base. Cut a notch at each leg position and fold the fabric around the leg before stapling. A non-woven fabric is easier to fold neatly around legs than a woven cambric.

A dust cover needs no maintenance. If it tears or sags, it can be pulled off and a new piece fitted in under an hour. The life of a cambric dust cover is indefinite if the chair is not dragged across hard floors: dragging catches the cambric on the floor surface and splits it along the staple line.

attrs.fix: Add extra staples at 25 mm centres along the sections that sag, pulling the cambric taut before each staple. The fabric should be drum-tight across the full underside.
attrs.cause: The fabric was not pulled taut before the perimeter staples were completed; it relaxed after the corner staples were fitted.
attrs.symptom: Cambric sags between staples after fitting

attrs.fix: Remove the loose staples and re-staple through a deeper fold. Drive each staple across the fold (perpendicular to the fabric edge) rather than along it to grip more of the fabric.
attrs.cause: The fold was not pressed firmly against the rail before stapling, or the staple did not catch enough of the fold depth.
attrs.symptom: Fold pulls away from the frame rail at one side
